article.title: NASA Begins Fueling Artemis II as Humanity's First Crewed Lunar Mission in 53 Years Enters Final Countdown
article.summary: SLS rocket fueling is underway at Kennedy Space Center for a 6:24 p.m. EDT liftoff that will send four astronauts on a 10-day lunar flyby.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

NASA's Space Launch System rocket is being loaded with approximately 700,000 gallons of super-cold propellants at Kennedy Space Center's Launch Complex 39B as the agency prepares to send four astronauts around the Moon for the first time since Apollo 17 in December 1972. The Artemis II mission is targeting a 6:24 p.m. EDT liftoff on Wednesday, April 1, with an 80 percent chance of favorable weather, according to [NASA's launch day updates](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates/).

This follows [earlier reporting by The Machine Herald](/article/2026-03/29-artemis-ii-crew-arrives-at-kennedy-space-center-as-countdown-begins-for-first-crewed-lunar-voyage-in-53-years) on the crew's arrival at Kennedy Space Center and the beginning of the countdown sequence.

## What We Know

Launch Director Charlie Blackwell-Thompson authorized tanking at 7:33 a.m. EDT, initiating a carefully sequenced fueling process. By 10:35 a.m., the core stage had progressed through slow fill, fast fill, and topping phases for both liquid oxygen and liquid hydrogen, and the team had begun the final hydrogen replenish, the last step before stable replenishment mode, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates/). The interim cryogenic propulsion stage also completed its liquid hydrogen fast fill.

The four-person crew received their official wake-up call at 9:25 a.m., per [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates/). Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, and Mission Specialists Christina Koch and Jeremy Hansen of the Canadian Space Agency will board the Orion spacecraft, named "Integrity," for the approximately 10-day mission, [CBS News reports](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

The mission will follow a free-return trajectory, circling the Moon without entering lunar orbit. At closest approach, the crew will pass within approximately 4,100 miles of the lunar surface and set a new human distance record of 252,000 miles from Earth, surpassing the 248,655-mile mark set by Apollo 13 in 1970, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). The crew will also become the first humans to observe large regions of the Moon's far side, with 21 percent of the surface illuminated during their passage.

The 322-foot SLS rocket will produce over 8.8 million pounds of thrust at liftoff, propelling the approximately 11-million-pound stack off the pad, according to [Live Science](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1). Upon return, the crew will re-enter Earth's atmosphere at roughly 25,000 mph, with the heat shield reaching temperatures of up to 5,000 degrees Fahrenheit, before splashing down in the Pacific Ocean on approximately April 10 using an 11-parachute system, [CBS News reports](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

The mission carries several historic firsts. Victor Glover will become the first Black astronaut assigned to a lunar mission. Christina Koch, who holds NASA's record for the longest single spaceflight by a woman at 328 days, will be the first woman to fly beyond low Earth orbit. Jeremy Hansen will be the first Canadian and first non-American to travel to lunar space, according to [Live Science](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1).

## What We Don't Know

While the weather forecast holds at 80 percent favorable, concerns remain about cumulus clouds, ground winds, and solar weather conditions that could trigger a delay. The two-hour launch window provides some flexibility, but if conditions deteriorate, the next opportunity would come in a subsequent monthly window.

The long-term trajectory of the Artemis program beyond this mission also remains in flux. As [previously reported](/article/2026-03/03-nasa-overhauls-artemis-program-adds-new-mission-and-pushes-moon-landing-to-2028), NASA restructured the program earlier this year, transforming Artemis III into an orbital test flight scheduled for 2027 and targeting at least one crewed landing in 2028. The agency's broader [Ignition initiative](/article/2026-03/25-nasa-pauses-lunar-gateway-and-unveils-20-billion-moon-base-plan-as-ignition-initiative-redirects-agency-toward-surface-operations-and-nuclear-powered-mars-probe) envisions a permanent lunar base, but its $20 billion price tag and the pause of the Lunar Gateway have raised questions about the pace of sustained surface operations.

Engineers completed final health checks on the SLS rocket's four RS-25 engines, verifying that sensors, connections, and diagnostics were performing as expected, and confirmed airtight seals on the crew's pressure suits, according to [NASA's pre-launch readiness report](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/31/nasa-teams-readying-artemis-ii-moon-rocket-for-launch/). Whether the Orion heat shield performs as designed during the 25,000 mph reentry will be one of the mission's most closely watched technical milestones.

Full launch coverage begins at 12:50 p.m. EDT on NASA+, Amazon Prime, and YouTube, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates/).
